sn35.28:1.1: At one time, the *Bhagavā* was dwelling at Gayā Head near Gayā, together with a thousand *bhikkhus*.
sn35.28:1.10: Burning with the fire of passion, with the fire of aversion, with the fire of delusion; burning with birth, old age, death, sorrows, lamentations, pains, distresses, and despairs, I say. …
sn35.28:1.11: The mind is burning, *dhammas* are burning, mind-consciousness [*manoviññāṇaṁ*] is burning, mind-contact is burning. And whatever is felt as pleasant or painful or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, arising dependent on mind-contact, that too is burning.
sn35.28:1.13: Burning with the fire of passion, with the fire of aversion, with the fire of delusion; burning with birth, old age, death, sorrows, lamentations, pains, distresses, and despairs, I say.
sn35.28:1.14: Seeing thus, *bhikkhus*, a learned noble disciple becomes disenchanted with the eye, becomes disenchanted with forms, becomes disenchanted with eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇepi*], becomes disenchanted with eye-contact. And whatever is felt as pleasant or painful or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, arising dependent on eye-contact, with that too one becomes disenchanted. …
sn35.28:1.15: And whatever is felt as pleasant or painful or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, arising dependent on mind-contact, with that too one becomes disenchanted.
sn35.28:1.16: Becoming disenchanted, one becomes dispassionate. Through dispassion, one is liberated. When liberated, there is the knowledge: ‘It is liberated.’
sn35.28:1.17: ‘Birth is ended, the holy life has been lived, what had to be done has been done, there is no more of this state of being,’ one understands.”
sn35.28:1.4: And what, *bhikkhus*, is everything that is burning?
sn35.28:1.5: The eye is burning, forms are burning, eye-consciousness [*cakkhuviññāṇaṁ*] is burning, eye-contact is burning. And whatever is felt as pleasant or painful or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, arising dependent on eye-contact, that too is burning.
sn35.28:1.7: Burning with the fire of passion, with the fire of aversion, with the fire of delusion; burning with birth, old age, death, sorrows, lamentations, pains, distresses, and despairs, I say. …
sn35.28:1.8: The tongue is burning, tastes are burning, tongue-consciousness [*jivhāviññāṇaṁ*] is burning, tongue-contact is burning. And whatever is felt as pleasant or painful or neither-painful-nor-pleasant, arising dependent on tongue-contact, that too is burning.
sn35.28:2.2: Satisfied, those *bhikkhus* delighted in what the *Bhagavā* had said.
sn35.28:2.3: And while this exposition was being spoken, through non-clinging [*anupādāya*], the minds of that thousand *bhikkhus* were liberated from the *āsavas*.
